49:## Commands
83:### Commands that will exist in later phases
139:## Plan-writing pitfalls

130	
   131	GitHub Actions' `ubuntu-latest` is now Ubuntu 24.04 with a system Python protected by [PEP 668](https://peps.python.org/pep-0668/) — bare `pip install` fails with `error: externally-managed-environment` instead of installing. Bash scripts that run on both CI (bash 5+) and contributor laptops (macOS ships bash 3.2 by default) hit the parallel constraint that bash-4-only builtins like `mapfile` / `readarray` silently work in CI but fail locally with `command not found`.
   132	
   133	When adding a new CI job that needs Python or runs bash scripts:
   134	
   135	+ **Python-using jobs must declare an interpreter via `actions/setup-python`** before any `pip install`. setup-python provisions an isolated interpreter that pip can write to freely, and adds it to PATH so a downstream `python3 …` resolves to the same interpreter the workflow installed into. Use `python3 -m pip install …`, not bare `pip install …`, to make the install/import binding explicit. The chunk-10e [`compose-e2e`](.github/workflows/ci.yml) job is the canonical small example: setup-python step, then `python3 -m pip install 'httpx>=0.27,<1'`, then a bash wrapper that calls `python3` on the driver. Jobs that don't need a custom Python (the existing `compose-smoke` etc. that only call `docker compose` / `jq` / `curl`) skip setup-python entirely.
   136	
   137	+ **Bash scripts under [`reference/compose/healthcheck/`](reference/compose/healthcheck/) and [`reference/scripts/`](reference/scripts/) must work under bash 3.2.** macOS' default `/bin/bash` is 3.2 and contributors run these scripts locally to repro CI failures. The two non-obvious incompatibilities to watch for: (1) `mapfile -t arr < <(cmd)` does not exist — use `arr=(); while IFS= read -r line; do [[ -n "$line" ]] && arr+=("$line"); done < <(cmd)` instead. (2) Associative arrays (`declare -A`) do not exist either; use a parallel-arrays pattern or a different data structure. `set -euo pipefail` and process substitution `< <(…)` both work fine in bash 3.2; only the named bash-4 builtins are off-limits.
   138	
   139	## Plan-writing pitfalls
   140	
   141	These are the recurring traps that have shown up across chunks 9c / 9d / 10d / 10e / 10d-followup-A / 10d-followup-B during `/codex-review`. Check each before submitting a plan that touches the orchestrator loop, the wire surface, or any role's submission flow.
   142	
   143	+ **Verify wire-touching shapes against the actual dataclasses, not the spec prose alone.** The submission types in [`reference/packages/eden-storage/src/eden_storage/submissions.py`](reference/packages/eden-storage/src/eden_storage/submissions.py) carry only `status` + role-specific id/payload fields — there is no free-form `description`. If your plan threads a `description` through the wire, you'll have to either drop it (route to host log only) or amend the spec + bindings + contracts together. Same discipline applies to event payloads: check [`reference/packages/eden-contracts/src/eden_contracts/event.py`](reference/packages/eden-contracts/src/eden_contracts/event.py) before assuming a field exists.
   144	+ **Any code path that produces a trial must run `Store.create_trial(status="starting")` before any observable repo write.** This is [`spec/v0/03-roles.md`](spec/v0/03-roles.md) §3.2 step 1, and the chunk-9c web-ui implementer at [`reference/services/web-ui/src/eden_web_ui/routes/implementer.py`](reference/services/web-ui/src/eden_web_ui/routes/implementer.py) is the canonical reference flow. Both the chunk-9c and 10d plans skipped the starting-trial step in their first revision until `/codex-review` caught it. The recovery path (chunk-1 expired-claim sweeper composite-commits the orphan trial to `error`) only works if the trial actually exists in `starting` state when the host crashes.
   145	
   146	+ **For compose-bound integration tests, audit which always-on services would race with the test.** The reference-host services (`planner-host`, `implementer-host`, `evaluator-host`) tight-loop-poll their respective task kinds on startup and claim *every* eligible task without filtering. So a test that wants to drive a task through the web UI cannot simply `compose up && stop <competing-host>` — by the time the stop returns, the competing host has already drained the seed. The chunk-10e [`e2e.sh`](reference/compose/healthcheck/e2e.sh) shows the right pattern: stage 1 brings up everything *except* the competing host, the test claims its targets via the UI, then stage 2 brings the competing host online so the rest of the experiment proceeds. Rule of thumb when planning: enumerate the polling services and ask "could the user's intent be drained before my test runs?" — if yes, defer the competing service to stage 2.
   147	
   148	+ **DooD wraps need explicit `name:` on every forwarded compose volume, and the spawned-child user identity must match the worker host's.** Two traps the 10d-followup-A implementation hit in succession during local-smoke debugging: (1) compose auto-prefixes volume names with the project (`eden-reference_eden-bare-repo`); a wrap that does `--mount source=eden-bare-repo` against the host docker daemon resolves to a *fresh empty volume* unless the compose volume has explicit `name:` matching the wrap's literal — see the `name:` discipline on [`compose.yaml`](reference/compose/compose.yaml) and [`compose.subprocess.yaml`](reference/compose/compose.subprocess.yaml). (2) The default container user differs between `eden-reference:dev` (eden:1000) and a freshly-pulled base image (root); spawned children that run as root against worktree files written by uid 1000 trip git's `dubious ownership` check, AND any commits they produce are uid-0-owned breaking subsequent integrator reads. The fix is to mirror the `eden:1000` user in `eden-runtime:dev` (the wrap deliberately doesn't pass `--user` so experiment-image USER overrides are honored). Rule of thumb when planning DooD: enumerate every forwarded volume and confirm `name:` is set; pin the spawned-child user to match the worker host's, not the spawned image's default.
   149	
   150	+ **DooD sibling containers survive SIGKILL of the local `docker run` client; the cidfile-driven `post_kill_callback` is load-bearing.** Killing the local `docker run` process via `os.killpg(SIGKILL)` does NOT propagate to the spawned container — the daemon parent keeps it running until the workload exits or it's killed externally. Plans that wrap `subprocess.Popen` with `docker run` must register a `post_kill_callback` (stored on the `Subprocess` instance, fires on the SIGKILL escalation branch) that reads the cidfile and runs `docker kill && docker rm -f`. The cleanup callback that unlinks the cidfile must run on **every** terminal exit branch (graceful, fast-path, and SIGKILL) — not just SIGKILL — because `--cidfile` errors if the path exists, so cidfile reuse without cleanup is a hard failure. See [`reference/services/_common/src/eden_service_common/container_exec.py`](reference/services/_common/src/eden_service_common/container_exec.py) and the §D.3 design notes in [`docs/plans/eden-phase-10d-followup-a-container-isolation.md`](docs/plans/eden-phase-10d-followup-a-container-isolation.md).
   151	
   152	+ **Three load-bearing wiring traps in any DooD sibling-container wrap.** The container-isolation chunk's CI burned through six rounds catching these in the wild; check each before submitting a plan that mounts the host docker socket into a worker. (1) **Privilege opt-in via separate overlay.** Putting `/var/run/docker.sock` mounts unconditionally in `compose.subprocess.yaml` would have granted DooD privilege to host-mode users by accident. The fix was a third overlay file ([`compose.docker-exec.yaml`](reference/compose/compose.docker-exec.yaml)) layered ON TOP of the subprocess overlay — that's the only file that mounts the socket and applies `group_add`. Both smoke scripts assert this directly via `docker inspect`. Rule of thumb: any deployment mode that needs new privilege (capability, host-network, socket mount, etc.) goes in its own compose overlay so the default keeps the privilege off. (2) **Paths in `docker run` flags resolve on the daemon side, NOT in the calling container's mount namespace.** `--cidfile <path>`, `--mount source=<path>`, `-v <host>:<container>` — all of these are interpreted on the host docker daemon, against the host filesystem. So a worker container that bind-mounts `${EDEN_CIDFILES_DIR_HOST}` at `/var/lib/eden/cidfiles` and passes `--cidfile /var/lib/eden/cidfiles/X.cid` will fail "permission denied" — that path doesn't exist on the host. The fix is to bind the cidfile dir at the SAME path inside the worker as on the host (`${EDEN_CIDFILES_DIR_HOST}:${EDEN_CIDFILES_DIR_HOST}`) so worker and daemon agree. Same posture for any path passed through `--mount source=`. (3) **`docker run -i` exits early on closed stdin.** Compose containers run with stdin closed by default; `docker run -i` propagates that closed-stdin to its parent's wait loop and exits *before* the workload has done useful work. Make `-i` conditional on whether the caller will pipe stdin to the spawn: planner subprocesses (long-running JSON-line protocol) need it; per-task implementer/evaluator spawns must NOT include it. See [`reference/services/_common/src/eden_service_common/container_exec.py`](reference/services/_common/src/eden_service_common/container_exec.py)'s `attach_stdin` parameter. (Bonus trap: `subprocess.Popen(shell=True)` runs `/bin/sh -c '<wrap>'`; on Linux that's typically dash, which exits on SIGTERM without forwarding to the docker run child. Prefix the wrap with `exec` so the shell `exec`s docker run in place — `popen.pid` then IS docker run.)
   153	
   154	+ **Any wire/network write that can leave server state unknown to the client needs a 3-outcome read-back ladder: confirmed-success / confirmed-divergence / indeterminate.** A `git push` that returns a transport error after the daemon may have already accepted the ref is the canonical example — there's no "did it land?" answer in the exit code alone. The 10d-followup-B integrator handles this by following the failed push with `ls_remote(trial.branch)` and routing on the three outcomes: remote==our-SHA → success (proceed to step 3), remote==different-SHA → divergence (compensating-delete local, raise `RefRefused`), remote-absent-or-unreadable → indeterminate (compensating-delete local, raise `IndeterminatePush`). Same shape as `StoreClient.integrate_trial`'s read-back from Phase 8a (chapter 07 §5). Rule of thumb when planning: any write to a remote system whose response can be lost in transit (HTTP `integrate_trial`, `git push`, future Postgres-over-network commits) needs a read-back step; do NOT collapse "transport error" into "definitely failed" — that would be a silent atomicity violation when the remote in fact accepted.
   155	
   156	+ **Recovery code must use spec-authoritative content paths, NOT ref-name parsing.** The 10d-followup-B remote-orphan reconciler needs to recover a `trial_id` from a remote-only ref (one that exists on Gitea but has no corresponding `Trial` record in the store). The first-cut plan parsed `refs/heads/trial/<id>-<slug>` with a regex; round-2 codex review caught that [`spec/v0/02-data-model.md`](spec/v0/02-data-model.md) §1.3 keeps `trial_id` opaque (any string ≤ 64 chars, the integrator's branch-name shape is non-normative). The fix walks the ref's commit tree and reads `.eden/trials/<id>/eval.json` — the spec-authoritative manifest path defined in chapter 6 §3.2. Rule of thumb when planning: anywhere recovery / reconciliation / GC code needs to extract a spec-defined identifier from a git artifact, the source of truth is the on-tree manifest the spec mandates, not the implementation's chosen branch-naming convention. Branch names are a reference-impl convenience.
   157	
   158	+ **Long-lived bare/non-bare clones need read-before-write/display fetches against the remote.** Two related round-0/round-1 findings on 10d-followup-B: (1) The admin work-refs page rendered from the local clone; if the integrator pushed a ref since the page-load, the admin's view was stale and the CAS-guarded delete-button could fail confusingly. The fix is `fetch_all_heads` immediately before reading refs to display, and `delete_remote_ref` BEFORE `delete_ref` so the remote-of-record is the source of truth. (2) The integrator's local clone may not have the work-branch the implementer pushed after orchestrator startup; an integrator that only fetches at startup misses any post-startup push. The fix is a per-promote-call `fetch_ref(trial.branch)` immediately before chapter-6 §2 reachability check. Rule of thumb when planning: any service that holds a clone of a remote-of-record must answer "when does this clone re-sync?" — at startup is rarely sufficient; the answer is usually "immediately before each operation that reads or writes a ref the remote could have changed."
   159	
   160	+ **Narrow exception handling on store reads when classifying authoritative-orphan vs indeterminate.** Impl round-0 on 10d-followup-B: `reconcile_remote_orphans` initially treated any `Exception` from `Store.read_trial(trial_id)` as "trial doesn't exist → orphan → delete the remote ref". A transient transport blip (StoreClient HTTP timeout, postgres connection drop) would have deleted legitimate in-flight integrator work. The fix narrows to `except NotFound: trial = None  # authoritative orphan` and routes any other exception to `continue  # indeterminate, leave the remote alone`. Rule of thumb when planning: anywhere a "missing → take destructive recovery action" branch is being written, the read that classifies "missing" must distinguish definitive `NotFound` from transport-indeterminate failure; collapsing the two is a silent data-loss vector.
   161	
   162	+ **Role-specific git/wire transport-failure mapping must match chapter 3 contracts.** Plan round-1 on 10d-followup-B: the first cut mapped any transport failure during git operations to a single failure mode. [`spec/v0/03-roles.md`](spec/v0/03-roles.md) actually differentiates by role — implementer transport failure on `push_ref` submits `status="error"` (chapter 3 §3.3); evaluator transport failure on `fetch_ref(trial.branch)` (preventing trial inspection) submits `status="eval_error"` (chapter 3 §4.4); integrator transport failure runs the chapter 6 §3.4 compensating-delete ladder (the integrator never "submits" — it composite-commits or rolls back). Rule of thumb when planning: when a wire/transport failure interrupts a role's flow, look up the failure-status vocabulary for THAT role in chapter 3 / chapter 6 — there is no shared "transport-failed" status across roles.
   163	
   164	+ **Tests for callbacks/lifecycle/atomicity must drive the actual code path through the public entry point, not just call the helper directly.** The chunk's first SIGKILL test called `kill_via_cidfile()` directly — it passed even though the production path through `Subprocess.terminate()`'s SIGKILL escalation branch was broken (the branch was being skipped because `popen.wait` returned success in 0.5s). Codex round-0 caught this; the fix was a rewrite that uses `eden_service_common.spawn(...)` + `sub.terminate(shutdown_deadline=0.5)` and asserts the spawned sibling container is gone after the public terminate call. See [`test_terminate_sigkill_path_invokes_post_kill_callback`](reference/services/_common/tests/test_container_exec_integration.py) for the canonical shape. Rule of thumb: when a callback is supposed to fire on a specific branch of a public method, the test must (a) construct the same setup the production caller would, (b) invoke the public method, and (c) assert the side effect. Helper-only tests can pass while the integrated path silently skips the branch.
   165	
   166	## Commit guidelines
   167	
   168	+ Short imperative subjects (e.g., "Add event protocol chapter", "Pin task state machine").
   169	+ For pull requests, include: what the change is, which phase it advances, and any spec ↔ impl implications.
   170	
   171	## Related docs
   172	
   173	+ [`docs/naming.md`](docs/naming.md) — what EDEN is, at the concept level.
   174	+ [`docs/roadmap.md`](docs/roadmap.md) — the 13-phase build-up plan with unit-level decomposition.
   175	+ [`docs/plans/`](docs/plans/) — active implementation plans.

item.text: I’ve found at least one plan-adherence drift in the citation tooling, and I’m checking the underlying spec sections now so I can separate “missing guardrail” from “test cites the wrong thing.”

1	# Wire Protocol — HTTP binding
     2	
     3	This chapter specifies an **HTTP binding** for the behaviors [chapter 4](04-task-protocol.md) (task state machine), [chapter 5](05-event-protocol.md) (event log), and [chapter 8](08-storage.md) (storage-side contract) already pin normatively. It does not re-specify those behaviors; every endpoint below maps 1:1 to a store-side operation named in those chapters and inherits that operation's semantics.
     4	
     5	A conforming EDEN deployment is **not required** to use this HTTP binding. The protocol constrains observable behavior; the transport that carries it is a deployment choice. This chapter exists so that HTTP-based deployments — including the Phase-8a reference deployment — can interoperate byte-for-byte.
     6	
     7	The binding intentionally omits the reference orchestrator's decision helpers (`validate_terminal`, `validate_metrics`). Those are reference-impl conveniences, not chapter-4/5/8 operations. A conforming third-party orchestrator is free to implement their decision logic inline; see §9.
     8	
     9	## 1. Transport
    10	
    11	### 1.1 HTTP version and content type
    12	
    13	A conforming server MUST accept HTTP/1.1 over TCP on a deployment-chosen host and port. HTTP/2 is OPTIONAL. Requests and responses that carry a body MUST use media type `application/json; charset=utf-8`, except for error responses which use `application/problem+json; charset=utf-8` (§6). Empty responses (e.g. 204) MUST NOT carry a body.
    14	
    15	### 1.2 Path versioning
    16	
    17	All normative paths are rooted at `/v0/`. Paths under `/_reference/` are explicitly non-normative and carry reference-implementation-specific behavior; a conforming client MUST NOT rely on them being present or on their semantics.
    18	
    19	Breaking changes to the binding produce a new root (`/v1/`), the same way breaking changes to the spec produce a new directory (`spec/v1/`). Non-breaking additions (new endpoints, new optional fields in existing payloads) stay under `/v0/`.
    20	
    21	### 1.3 Experiment scoping
    22	
    23	Every normative path segment below `/v0/` begins with `experiments/{experiment_id}/`, and every request MUST additionally send the header `X-Eden-Experiment-Id: {experiment_id}` with a value equal to the path segment. A server MUST reject a request whose header disagrees with its path segment with `eden://error/experiment-id-mismatch` (HTTP 400); see §6. The header is defense-in-depth against misrouted clients and proxies.
    24	
    25	## 2. Task operations (chapter 4)
    26	
    27	The following endpoints bind the task-store operations in [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §§1–5 and [`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §1.1. Each endpoint inherits the normative semantics of the underlying operation; this section lists only the wire shape.
    28	
    29	| Operation | HTTP | Path |
    30	|---|---|---|
    31	| `create_task` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks` |
    32	| `list_tasks` | `GET`  | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks` |
    33	| `read_task` | `GET`  | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}` |
    34	| `read_submission` | `GET`  | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/submission` |
    35	| `claim` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/claim` |
    36	| `submit` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/submit` |
    37	| `accept` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/accept` |
    38	| `reject` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/reject` |
    39	| `reclaim` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/reclaim` |
    40	
    41	### 2.1 Create
    42	
    43	`POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks` accepts a JSON request body whose shape matches [`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json) with `state == "pending"` and no `claim`. On success the server returns 200 with the created task as the response body (same schema). The composite-commit rules in [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §2.2 apply: creating an `implement` task transitions the referenced proposal atomically with the task insert; the server MUST perform both effects in a single transaction.
    44	
    45	### 2.2 List and read
    46	
    47	- `GET /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks` returns an array of tasks matching optional query parameters `kind` (one of `plan`, `implement`, `evaluate`) and `state` (one of the five task states). Ordering is implementation-defined per [`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §1.1.
    48	- `GET /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}` returns the task object with id `T`, or 404 `eden://error/not-found`.
    49	
    50	### 2.3 Claim
    51	
    52	`POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/claim` accepts a JSON body with at minimum the field `worker_id` and an OPTIONAL `expires_at` timestamp. On success the server returns 200 with a `claim` object matching the shape from [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4 (see also [`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json)) — notably including the fresh `token`. On a non-`pending` task the server returns 409 `eden://error/illegal-transition`. The claim-token secrecy rule ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §3.2) implies that intermediary proxies and logs MUST NOT persist claim tokens in plaintext; that is a deployment concern and the binding does not enforce it.
    53	
    54	### 2.4 Submit
    55	
    56	`POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/submit` accepts a JSON body containing the current `token` and a role-specific `payload` (matching the claim's task `kind`). Atomicity, idempotency, and content-equivalence rules from [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4 apply unchanged. A resubmit with a content-equivalent payload MUST return 200; a resubmit with a divergent payload MUST return 409 `eden://error/conflicting-resubmission`. A wrong token MUST return 403 `eden://error/wrong-token`.
    57	
    58	### 2.5 Accept and reject
    59	
    60	- `POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/accept` transitions `submitted → completed` per [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3. The request body is empty (the orchestrator has already persisted the submission; the server re-reads it to apply the composite-commit effects).
    61	- `POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/reject` transitions `submitted → failed` per [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3. The request body carries a single field `reason` drawn from the closed v0 vocabulary ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §3.1): `"worker_error"`, `"validation_error"`, or `"policy_limit"`.
    62	
    63	### 2.6 Reclaim
    64	
    65	`POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/reclaim` transitions a `claimed` or (operator-invoked) `submitted` task back to `pending` per [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §5. The request body carries a single field `cause` drawn from the closed v0 vocabulary: `"expired"`, `"operator"`, or `"health_policy"`.
    66	
    67	## 3. Proposal operations
    68	
    69	| Operation | HTTP | Path |
    70	|---|---|---|
    71	| `create_proposal` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/proposals` |
    72	| `list_proposals` | `GET` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/proposals` |
    73	| `read_proposal` | `GET` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/proposals/{P}` |
    74	| `mark_proposal_ready` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/proposals/{P}/mark-ready` |
    75	
    76	Request and response bodies match [`schemas/proposal.schema.json`](schemas/proposal.schema.json). `list_proposals` accepts an optional `state` query parameter. `mark-ready` has an empty request body.
    77	
    78	## 4. Trial operations
    79	
    80	| Operation | HTTP | Path |
    81	|---|---|---|
    82	| `create_trial` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/trials` |
    83	| `list_trials` | `GET` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/trials` |
    84	| `read_trial` | `GET` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}` |
    85	| `declare_trial_eval_error` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}/declare-eval-error` |
    86	| `integrate_trial` | `POST` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}/integrate` |
    87	
    88	`integrate_trial` binds [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §3.4 and carries additional idempotency rules (§5 below); the other endpoints are transport-only bindings of their [`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §1.7 operations.
    89	
    90	## 5. `integrate_trial` — same-value idempotency
    91	
    92	The `POST /v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}/integrate` endpoint has additional semantics beyond transport-binding because [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §3.4's atomicity invariant spans the process boundary. The request body carries the single field `trial_commit_sha`.
    93	
    94	- **Success (first call).** The server atomically writes `trial_commit_sha` on the trial and appends `trial.integrated` per [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §3.4. Response: 200, empty body.
    95	- **Same-value idempotency.** A repeated call whose `trial_commit_sha` equals the value already stored on the trial MUST return 200 and MUST NOT append a second `trial.integrated` event. This is what lets a client safely retry an `integrate_trial` request after a transport- indeterminate failure without double-commit.
    96	- **Different-value rejection.** A call whose `trial_commit_sha` differs from the value already stored MUST return 409 `eden://error/invalid-precondition`. A conforming client MUST surface this as an atomicity violation (the chapter 6 §1.2 sole-writer rule has been violated somewhere upstream); operator intervention is required.
    97	- **Other preconditions.** Standard [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §3.4 preconditions continue to apply: the trial MUST be in `status == "success"`, and the `trial_commit_sha` MUST be a well-formed commit SHA; violations return `invalid-precondition`.
    98	
    99	A client that issues `integrate_trial` and does not receive a 2xx response (connection reset, read timeout, proxy disconnect) MUST treat the outcome as indeterminate and MUST NOT assume the server has not committed. The reference reconciliation procedure is a read-back (`GET /v0/experiments/{E}/trials/{T}`) that resolves to one of three outcomes:
   100	
   101	- observed `trial_commit_sha` equals the sent value → treat as success;
   102	- observed `trial_commit_sha` is set but differs → raise the same atomicity-violation error the server would have;
   103	- observed `trial_commit_sha` is absent, or the read-back itself fails → raise an indeterminate-result error. Absence does **not** prove the request failed; it may still be in flight.
   104	
   105	## 6. Event log
   106	
   107	| Operation | HTTP | Path |
   108	|---|---|---|
   109	| `read_range` / `replay` | `GET` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/events` |
   110	| `subscribe` | `GET` | `/v0/experiments/{E}/events/subscribe` |
   111	
   112	### 6.1 Read-range and replay
   113	
   114	`GET /v0/experiments/{E}/events` returns the log in order. It accepts one query parameter:
   115	
   116	- `cursor` — cumulative count of events the caller has already observed. Omitted or `0` triggers `replay` per [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4.4.
   117	
   118	The response is `{"events": [...], "cursor": N}` where `events` is the delivered batch (possibly empty) and `cursor` is the new cumulative count the caller should pass to the next call. The endpoint does not block; it returns whatever is available immediately.
   119	
   120	### 6.2 Long-poll subscribe
   121	
   122	`GET /v0/experiments/{E}/events/subscribe` binds the chapter 8 §2.1 `subscribe` operation as an HTTP long-poll. It accepts the same `cursor` query parameter. The server holds the connection open until one or more events are available after `cursor`, then returns them. If no event arrives within a server-chosen timeout (RECOMMENDED: 30 seconds), the server returns an empty batch so the caller can reconnect. The response body shape is identical to §6.1.
   123	
   124	Subscribers iterate this endpoint in a loop, advancing `cursor` by the length of each returned batch. The at-least-once and total- order guarantees from [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §4 are preserved because the underlying `read_range` semantics preserve them.
   125	
   126	Push transports (Server-Sent Events, WebSocket) are OPTIONAL alternatives a deployment MAY expose alongside long-poll. They do not change the normative `subscribe` contract.
   127	
   128	## 7. Error envelope
   129	
   130	Every non-2xx response MUST use media type `application/problem+json; charset=utf-8` with a body matching [`schemas/wire/error.schema.json`](schemas/wire/error.schema.json):
   131	
   132	```json
   133	{
   134	  "type":     "eden://error/<name>",
   135	  "title":    "<short human phrase>",
   136	  "status":   <HTTP status>,
   137	  "detail":   "<longer human-readable message>",
   138	  "instance": "<request URL>"
   139	}
   140	```
   141	
   142	The `type` URI is the authoritative machine-readable error code. Clients MUST key retry and recovery on `type`, not on HTTP status alone (several conflict-class errors share 409). The closed v0 vocabulary is:
   143	
   144	| `type` | HTTP | When |
   145	|---|---|---|
   146	| `eden://error/bad-request` | 400 | request body fails schema validation |
   147	| `eden://error/experiment-id-mismatch` | 400 | header-vs-path experiment-ID disagreement (§1.3) |
   148	| `eden://error/wrong-token` | 403 | presented claim token ≠ stored token ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §3.3) |
   149	| `eden://error/not-found` | 404 | referenced entity does not exist |
   150	| `eden://error/already-exists` | 409 | insert collided with existing id |
   151	| `eden://error/illegal-transition` | 409 | state-machine violation |
   152	| `eden://error/conflicting-resubmission` | 409 | resubmit disagreed with committed payload ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.2) |
   153	| `eden://error/invalid-precondition` | 409 | referenced entity not in required state; also the different-SHA branch of `integrate_trial` (§5) |
   154	
   155	A server MUST NOT emit a `type` outside this vocabulary for a v0 endpoint. Implementations MAY add custom error types at non-`/v0/` paths (e.g. `/_reference/...`) using the same envelope.
   156	
   157	## 8. Idempotency and retry
   158	
   159	### 8.1 Submit
   160	
   161	The [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.2 submit-idempotency rule is preserved by the binding: a retry of `POST /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/submit` carrying the same token and content-equivalent payload MUST return 200 identically to the original call. This lets a worker retry a submit after a transport error without risk of double- advance. No HTTP-level `Idempotency-Key` header is required.
   162	
   163	### 8.2 Integrate
   164	
   165	Same-value idempotency on `integrate_trial` is pinned in §5.
   166	
   167	### 8.3 Other mutations
   168	
   169	Claim, reject, reclaim, and accept are **not** blindly retry-safe on transport failures. A retry after the server already committed the transition will return `illegal-transition`, but that error is not uniquely distinguishable from a racing actor or a reclaim that interleaved between attempts. The binding does **not** standardize an HTTP-level retry convention for these endpoints; a client MUST resolve ambiguous failures via a follow-up `GET /v0/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}` read and apply operation- specific knowledge. A future spec lineage MAY introduce per- operation idempotency tokens.
   170	
   171	## 9. Reference-only helpers (non-normative)
   172	
   173	The reference `eden_wire` server also exposes:
   174	
   175	- `GET /_reference/experiments/{E}/tasks/{T}/validate-terminal`
   176	- `POST /_reference/experiments/{E}/validate/metrics`
   177	
   178	These are conveniences for the Phase-5 dispatch driver and are **not** part of the normative binding. A conforming third-party client MUST NOT rely on them being present. A conforming third-party orchestrator implementing its own accept/reject decision inline is free to do so; the [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3 decision rules are all that matter for the state machine.
   179	
   180	Future phases MAY promote individual helpers into the normative binding if conformance testing requires it; such promotions follow the same versioning discipline as any other binding change.
   181	
   182	## 10. Conformance
   183	
   184	A conforming server:
   185	
   186	- Implements §§2–7 with the normative semantics of [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md), [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md), [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §3.4, and [`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md).
   187	- Exposes `/v0/` with the exact URL shapes listed above.
   188	- Returns problem+json with `type` from the §7 vocabulary on every non-2xx response.
   189	- Preserves the task-protocol, event-protocol, and integrator-atomicity invariants unchanged by the HTTP transport.
   190	
   191	A conforming client:
   192	
   193	- Keys retry and recovery on the `type` URI, not on HTTP status alone.
   194	- Resolves transport-indeterminate `integrate_trial` failures via the read-back procedure in §5 and does not compensate ref writes without positive confirmation of server-side non-commit.
   195	- Does not depend on `/_reference/` endpoints.
   196	
   197	## 11. Implementation latitude
   198	
   199	The binding leaves to implementations:
   200	
   201	- The concrete HTTP server (FastAPI, Starlette, bespoke); the reference impl uses FastAPI.
   202	- The SSE/WebSocket push transport, if any.
   203	- The RECOMMENDED long-poll timeout; the reference impl uses 30 seconds.
   204	- Transport-level concerns outside the binding's scope (TLS, compression, authentication). Authentication scaffolding is a later-phase concern.
   205	
   206	What the binding does **not** leave to implementations:
   207	
   208	- The URL shapes (§§2–6).
   209	- The problem+json error vocabulary (§7).
   210	- The same-value idempotency on `integrate_trial` (§5).
   211	- Preservation of the [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md), [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md), [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §3.4, and [`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) invariants through the HTTP transport.
   212	
   213	## 12. Reference-only extensions — authentication (informative)
   214	
   215	§11 restates that authentication is outside the normative binding. The reference implementation in [`reference/packages/eden-wire/`](../../reference/packages/eden-wire/) ships an optional shared-token check: when configured, the server requires `Authorization: Bearer <token>` on every request and rejects others with a problem+json response whose `type` is `eden://reference-error/unauthorized` and `status` is 401.
   216	
   217	Conforming servers MAY adopt this scheme, adopt a different scheme, or require no authentication at all. The only constraint §7 imposes on any authentication scheme is that it MUST NOT emit error `type` values inside the normative `eden://error/…` namespace — the v0 vocabulary there is closed, and overloading one of those values with an authentication outcome would force clients to disambiguate auth rejections from store-state rejections on HTTP status alone.
   218	
   219	This section is informative; it does not extend the closed error vocabulary in §7.

1	# Event Protocol
     2	
     3	This chapter specifies the event log: the envelope every event MUST carry, the transactional invariant that binds event writes to the state changes they describe, the per-type payload shapes for the v0 event registry, and the delivery guarantees a conforming event log MUST offer to subscribers.
     4	
     5	The event envelope's JSON Schema is [`schemas/event.schema.json`](schemas/event.schema.json). The behavioral contracts that produce events are in [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) (task transitions) and [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) (trial promotion). The event log's durability and subscription semantics as a *store* are in [`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md); this chapter specifies what events mean, not how they are persisted.
     6	
     7	## 1. Envelope
     8	
     9	Every event appended to a conforming event log MUST be a JSON object carrying:
    10	
    11	| Field | Required | Type | Meaning |
    12	|---|---|---|---|
    13	| `event_id` | yes | string | Unique identifier for this event within the log. Opaque. |
    14	| `type` | yes | string | Dotted type name (`^[a-z][a-z0-9_]*(\.[a-z][a-z0-9_]*)+$`). The normative registry is in §3. |
    15	| `occurred_at` | yes | timestamp | When the state change happened. UTC, trailing `Z`, RFC 3339 profile (`^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}(\.[0-9]+)?Z$`). |
    16	| `experiment_id` | yes | string | The experiment the event belongs to. Events are scoped per experiment ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §4.3). |
    17	| `data` | yes | object | Type-specific payload. Shape is fixed by the registry entry for `type` (§3). |
    18	
    19	The envelope is closed over these fields for conforming events; a conforming log MAY persist additional audit metadata alongside each event, but MUST NOT expose it under any of the five envelope keys. Implementations MAY include additional top-level keys outside the envelope (e.g. `sequence`, `recorded_at`); subscribers relying only on the envelope MUST remain correct regardless.
    20	
    21	### 1.1 `event_id` uniqueness
    22	
    23	`event_id` MUST be unique within the log. A subscriber MAY dedupe by `event_id` when recovering from a disconnect.
    24	
    25	### 1.2 `occurred_at` vs. log order
    26	
    27	`occurred_at` records when the state change happened, which MAY differ from the event's position in the log — clock skew, batched writes, or retried appends can all produce non-monotonic `occurred_at` within an experiment's stream. Causal order is established by the log itself (§4.3), not by timestamps. `occurred_at` is advisory for human debugging; subscribers MUST NOT rely on it for ordering.
    28	
    29	## 2. Transactional invariant
    30	
    31	The central invariant of the event protocol, stated in [`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §6:
    32	
    33	> **Every state change that is observable via tasks, proposals, or > trials MUST be accompanied by a corresponding event, and the event > write MUST be atomic with the state change it describes.**
    34	
    35	"Atomic" means: either both the state change and the event append are durable, or neither is. A conforming implementation MUST NOT expose a state change to readers (direct store reads, cached projections, push notifications, role dispatch) before its event is durable in the log; conversely, it MUST NOT append an event whose state change has not been durably written. If a transaction covering both is aborted, neither is observable.
    36	
    37	The invariant applies to the set of entities enumerated in [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md): tasks, proposals, trials. Implementations MAY emit *additional* events outside this set (see §3.5) that do not correspond to a protocol-defined state change; those additional events are not subject to the transactional invariant because there is no state change to bind them to.
    38	
    39	### 2.1 Why atomic
    40	
    41	The event log is the normative observability channel ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §6.3). Without atomicity, subscribers reconstructing history can observe either a state without its event or an event whose state the store does not yet expose — either is a protocol violation that leaves subscribers unable to trust the log.
    42	
    43	### 2.2 Composite transitions
    44	
    45	Several transitions span multiple entities and MUST commit together:
    46	
    47	- **Implement dispatch** — creating a `task` with `kind=implement` and transitioning its referenced `proposal` from `ready` to `dispatched` ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §2). Events: `task.created` + `proposal.dispatched`, in one atomic commit.
    48	- **Implement terminal** — the `implement` task's terminal transition (`submitted → completed` or `submitted → failed`) and the matching `proposal` transition from `dispatched` to `completed` ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3, §7). Events: `task.completed` (or `task.failed`) + `proposal.completed`, in one atomic commit.
    49	- **Evaluate terminal (`success`/`error`)** — the `evaluate` task's terminal transition plus writes to the trial's `status`, `metrics`, `artifacts_uri`, and `completed_at` ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §4.4). Events: `task.completed` (or `task.failed`) + `trial.succeeded` / `trial.errored`, in one atomic commit.
    50	- **Implement reclaim with in-flight trial** — reclamation of an `implement` task whose prior execution left a trial in `starting` requires transitioning that trial to `error` atomically with the reclaim ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §5.4). Events: `task.reclaimed` + `trial.errored`, in one atomic commit.
    51	- **Retry-exhausted `eval_error` terminal** — the orchestrator's transition of a trial from `starting` to `eval_error` ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3). When the orchestrator persists this transition as a state change on the trial, it MUST emit `trial.eval_errored` atomically.
    52	- **Trial promotion** — the integrator's write of a `trial/*` commit and the `trial_commit_sha` field on the trial ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)). Event: `trial.integrated`.
    53	
    54	A subscriber processing any of these composite events MUST therefore either observe the full set or observe none; partial visibility is a protocol violation.
    55	
    56	### 2.3 What is *not* a state change
    57	
    58	Operations that do not change protocol-owned state do not require events:
    59	
    60	- Reading a task, proposal, trial, or event.
    61	- Uploading artifacts to the artifact store before they are referenced from a protocol-owned object. (Populating `artifacts_uri` on a proposal or trial *is* a state change and is covered by the events on those objects.)
    62	- Worker-internal progress (scratch files, local logs).
    63	
    64	Implementations MAY expose operational telemetry through other channels without running it through the event log.
    65	
    66	## 3. Event registry
    67	
    68	Every v0 event `type` defined by the protocol is listed below. For each, the `data` object's required fields are pinned; optional fields are called out per entry. The JSON Schema in [`schemas/event.schema.json`](schemas/event.schema.json) enforces these shapes via `if/then` dispatch on `type`.
    69	
    70	Implementations MAY emit additional event types outside this registry (§3.5). The `type` pattern in §1 is the only structural constraint on them.
    71	
    72	### 3.1 Task events
    73	
    74	Produced atomically with the transitions defined in [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). The `task_id` field on every task-event payload names the task whose transition is being recorded.
    75	
    76	| Type | Transition | `data` required fields |
    77	|---|---|---|
    78	| `task.created` | — → `pending` | `task_id`, `kind` |
    79	| `task.claimed` | `pending` → `claimed` | `task_id`, `worker_id` |
    80	| `task.submitted` | `claimed` → `submitted` | `task_id` |
    81	| `task.completed` | `submitted` → `completed` | `task_id` |
    82	| `task.failed` | `submitted` → `failed` | `task_id`, `reason` |
    83	| `task.reclaimed` | `claimed`/`submitted` → `pending` | `task_id`, `cause` |
    84	
    85	Payload field definitions:
    86	
    87	- `task_id` — the `task_id` of the transitioning task.
    88	- `kind` — the task's `kind` ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.1); one of `plan`, `implement`, `evaluate`.
    89	- `worker_id` — the `claim.worker_id` recorded on the successful claim ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §3.2).
    90	- `reason` — one of the strings `"worker_error"` (the worker's submission declared failure), `"validation_error"` (the orchestrator rejected the result as malformed or non-conforming), or `"policy_limit"` (a policy such as retry budget caused the failure). The literal set is closed for v0; an implementation that needs finer granularity MAY add a separate operator-level event under its own type (§3.5).
    91	- `cause` — one of `"expired"` (claim `expires_at` passed), `"operator"` (explicit operator action), or `"health_policy"` (task store health policy declared the worker unreachable). The literal set is closed for v0 on the same terms as `reason`.
    92	
    93	The payload MAY include additional fields beyond those required; subscribers MUST tolerate them. A conforming orchestrator SHOULD include the submitting worker's `worker_id` on `task.submitted`, `task.completed`, and `task.failed` events when known, as an operational convenience — but this is not required because the worker-task binding is already recoverable from the preceding `task.claimed` event.
    94	
    95	### 3.2 Proposal events
    96	
    97	Produced atomically with the proposal `state` transitions defined in [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5 and [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.2. Composite commits that bind a proposal event to a task event are enumerated in §2.2.
    98	
    99	| Type | Transition | `data` required fields |
   100	|---|---|---|
   101	| `proposal.drafted` | — → `drafting` | `proposal_id` |
   102	| `proposal.ready` | `drafting` → `ready` | `proposal_id` |
   103	| `proposal.dispatched` | `ready` → `dispatched` | `proposal_id`, `task_id` |
   104	| `proposal.completed` | `dispatched` → `completed` | `proposal_id`, `task_id` |
   105	
   106	Payload field definitions:
   107	
   108	- `proposal_id` — the `proposal_id` of the transitioning proposal.
   109	- `task_id` — for `proposal.dispatched`, the `task_id` of the `implement` task created by the same commit (§2.2). For `proposal.completed`, the `task_id` of the `implement` task whose terminal transition is being recorded.
   110	
   111	### 3.3 Trial events
   112	
   113	Produced atomically with trial `status` transitions and with the integrator's promotion write. The trial's lifecycle is defined in [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7 and [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §3–§4; the promotion step is in [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).
   114	
   115	| Type | Transition | `data` required fields |
   116	|---|---|---|
   117	| `trial.started` | — → `starting` | `trial_id`, `proposal_id` |
   118	| `trial.succeeded` | `starting` → `success` | `trial_id`, `commit_sha` |
   119	| `trial.errored` | `starting` → `error` | `trial_id` |
   120	| `trial.eval_errored` | `starting` → `eval_error` | `trial_id` |
   121	| `trial.integrated` | — (integrator writes `trial_commit_sha`) | `trial_id`, `trial_commit_sha` |
   122	
   123	Payload field definitions:
   124	
   125	- `trial_id` — the `trial_id` of the transitioning trial.
   126	- `proposal_id` — the trial's `proposal_id` ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1).
   127	- `commit_sha` — the worker-branch tip recorded on the trial at the moment of success ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7.1).
   128	- `trial_commit_sha` — the canonical-lineage SHA the integrator wrote ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)). A 40-hex SHA-1 or a 64-hex SHA-256; the same pattern as commits elsewhere in the data model.
   129	
   130	`trial.integrated` is not a trial-`status` transition — integration does not change the trial's `status` field, only its `trial_commit_sha`. The event marks promotion so subscribers can reconstruct the canonical lineage without reading git directly.
   131	
   132	### 3.4 Subscriber observability guarantee
   133	
   134	Every protocol-owned state-machine transition in v0 is covered by exactly one event type in §3.1–§3.3. A subscriber that consumes every event with a registered `type`, in log order, MUST be able to reconstruct the **lifecycle history** of every task, proposal, and trial in the experiment: which entities exist, in what states, in what order. A conforming implementation MUST NOT expose a state- machine transition that is not marked by its corresponding registered event.
   135	
   136	Registered event payloads carry only the fields needed to *identify* the transitioning entity and any cross-entity references (e.g. `proposal.dispatched` carries the implement `task_id`). They do not carry full entity snapshots. A subscriber that needs the content of an entity (the proposal's `parent_commits` and `artifacts_uri`, the trial's `metrics` and `completed_at`) MUST read that entity from its store using the identifier the event carries; the read returns the entity's current state ([`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §1.7). This boundary is deliberate: events mark what happened; the entity stores hold what the entity *is*. Coupling the two into a full event-sourced projection — a subscriber reconstructing every intermediate entity value from events alone — is a deployment MAY implement on top of v0 but is not a protocol requirement, since v0 does not mandate historical reads on the entity stores.
   137	
   138	The transactional invariant (§2) combined with the atomic event + state-change rule guarantees that any entity reachable via an event's identifier is already durable at read time.
   139	
   140	### 3.5 Non-registered event types
   141	
   142	Implementations MAY emit events whose `type` is not listed in §3.1–§3.3, provided the type conforms to the `^[a-z][a-z0-9_]*(\.[a-z][a-z0-9_]*)+$` pattern. Such events carry no protocol-defined semantics and MUST NOT be required by any conforming subscriber.
   143	
   144	A non-registered event MUST NOT carry a registered `type` with a non-conforming payload; that is a protocol violation, not an extension. The registry is closed at v0: a future spec lineage (`v1`) MAY add or revise registered types.
   145	
   146	## 4. Delivery guarantees
   147	
   148	A conforming event log MUST provide each subscriber the following guarantees, which subscribers MAY rely on without additional coordination.
   149	
   150	### 4.1 Total order per experiment
   151	
   152	Within a single experiment, a conforming event log MUST present a single total order to subscribers. Two subscribers reading the same experiment's stream MUST observe every pair of events in the same relative order. The ordering mechanism (monotonic sequence, log position, vector clock) is a storage concern; the observable guarantee is not.
   153	
   154	### 4.2 Causal consistency
   155	
   156	Within the per-experiment total order, the sequence MUST respect causality: if event B describes a state change that depended on a state recorded by event A, A MUST precede B in the log ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §4.3). In particular, for every composite commit enumerated in §2.2, the events produced by that commit MAY appear in any order relative to each other — they were written atomically and neither causes the other — but they MUST all precede any event that causally depends on the committed state.
   157	
   158	### 4.3 At-least-once delivery
   159	
   160	A conforming log MUST deliver every appended event to every active subscriber at least once. A subscriber MAY observe the same `event_id` more than once under recovery, restart, or reconnect; subscribers MUST dedupe by `event_id` when exact-once semantics are required for their projection.
   161	
   162	Implementations MAY offer stronger delivery than at-least-once (e.g. exactly-once via offset commits), but MUST NOT offer weaker than at-least-once.
   163	
   164	### 4.4 Replayability
   165	
   166	For the **lifetime of an experiment** — from registration until the experiment reaches its operator-declared terminal state (Phase 12 control-plane concern; for v0, treat this as "until the deployment explicitly archives the experiment") — a conforming log MUST retain every appended event for that experiment and MUST allow any subscriber to replay the full stream from the experiment's first event. This is what lets late subscribers reconstruct history and lets existing subscribers recover from projection corruption. The log MAY allow starting from a caller-supplied position (event ID or offset) as an optimization, but the complete replay is required.
   167	
   168	A conforming deployment MAY compact or archive an experiment's events *only* after the experiment has reached its operator-declared terminal state. Once compacted, the log MUST either preserve full replay from an archive (preferred), or reject replays older than the compaction horizon with a well-defined error. A log MUST NOT silently drop events from a replay during an experiment's active lifetime.
   169	
   170	### 4.5 Durability
   171	
   172	A successful append MUST be durable through the next crash of the log implementation. An event the log has acknowledged to the writer MUST NOT later disappear from any subscriber's stream. Durability requirements for the log as a store are pinned in [`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §3.
   173	
   174	### 4.6 No cross-experiment ordering
   175	
   176	Events in different experiments have no mandated relative order. A conforming log MAY expose a global ordering (e.g. a single monotonic sequence across all experiments) as an implementation convenience, but a subscriber that relies on cross-experiment order is relying on something the protocol does not guarantee.
   177	
   178	## 5. Implementation latitude
   179	
   180	The protocol leaves to implementations:
   181	
   182	- The transport and storage of events (append-only table, commit log, message broker).
   183	- The subscription mechanism (long-poll, push, WebSocket, `LISTEN`).
   184	- The concrete `event_id` representation, as long as uniqueness (§1.1) holds.
   185	- The retention policy — a log MAY compact or archive after a policy window, provided replay (§4.4) is preserved for the retained window. Consumers that need history beyond the retention policy's horizon are responsible for their own durable projections.
   186	- The mechanism of atomicity (§2): transactional database, outbox pattern, two-phase commit across task store and log, or a unified store where the log *is* the task store's write-ahead record.
   187	
   188	What the protocol does **not** leave to implementations:
   189	
   190	- The envelope fields (§1).
   191	- The transactional invariant (§2).
   192	- The registered event types and their payloads (§3.1–§3.3).
   193	- The delivery guarantees in §4.

1	# Task Protocol
     2	
     3	This chapter specifies the behavioral contract for tasks: the state machine they advance through, the claim tokens that grant exclusive execution, the idempotency rules for submission, and the reclamation policy that bounds worker liveness. It pins *what* observable behavior a conforming task store and a conforming orchestrator must exhibit; it does not pin *how* either is implemented.
     4	
     5	The task object's shape is defined in [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3; its JSON Schema is [`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json). The role contracts that drive the transitions defined here are in [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md).
     6	
     7	## 1. State machine
     8	
     9	### 1.1 States
    10	
    11	A task's `state` field takes one of five values. Each has a well-defined meaning in the state machine:
    12	
    13	| State | Meaning |
    14	|---|---|
    15	| `pending` | The task has been created and is eligible to be claimed. No worker currently holds it. |
    16	| `claimed` | A worker holds an active claim on the task and is expected to execute it. The task carries a `claim` object. |
    17	| `submitted` | The claiming worker has presented a result. The task awaits processing by the orchestrator. |
    18	| `completed` | The orchestrator has accepted the result. The task is terminal. |
    19	| `failed` | The orchestrator has rejected the result, the worker declared failure, or a policy limit (e.g. retry budget) has been exceeded. The task is terminal. |
    20	
    21	`completed` and `failed` are **terminal**: a conforming task store MUST NOT transition a task out of either state.
    22	
    23	### 1.2 Transitions
    24	
    25	The permitted transitions are exactly:
    26	
    27	```text
    28	          claim                 submit                accept
    29	pending ─────────▶ claimed ─────────────▶ submitted ─────────▶ completed
    30	   ▲                 │                       │
    31	   │ reclaim         │ reclaim               │ reject
    32	   └─────────────────┴───────────────────────┤           ──▶ failed
    33	                                             │
    34	```
    35	
    36	- **claim** — `pending → claimed`. Atomic. Issues a fresh claim token. §3.
    37	- **submit** — `claimed → submitted`. Requires presenting the current claim token. Idempotent under identical payload. Performed for every completed attempt, whether the worker declares success or failure. §4.
    38	- **reclaim** — `claimed → pending` or `submitted → pending`. Invalidates the prior claim token. §5.
    39	- **accept** — `submitted → completed`. Orchestrator-initiated. §4.3.
    40	- **reject** — `submitted → failed`. Orchestrator-initiated, including when the worker's own submission declared failure. §4.3.
    41	
    42	Any transition not listed above MUST be rejected by the task store. A conforming task store MUST treat concurrent transition attempts as serialized: whichever attempt wins the race succeeds, and the others MUST receive a well-defined rejection that lets the caller distinguish "invalid" from "raced".
    43	
    44	### 1.3 Event emission
    45	
    46	Every state transition MUST be accompanied by a corresponding event appended to the event log, atomically with the state change. The event types (e.g. `task.claimed`, `task.submitted`, `task.failed`) and the transactional invariant are specified in [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md). This chapter does not enumerate the event shapes; it requires only that they exist and that no state change is observable without its event.
    47	
    48	## 2. Creating a task
    49	
    50	A task enters the system in the `pending` state. A conforming orchestrator MUST:
    51	
    52	- Populate `task_id`, `kind`, `state = "pending"`, `payload`, `created_at`, `updated_at` ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.1).
    53	- Ensure the payload satisfies the dispatch preconditions for the task's `kind`:
    54	  - `plan` — `payload.experiment_id` names an experiment the planner is authorized to work on.
    55	  - `implement` — `payload.proposal_id` names a proposal with `state == "ready"` at creation time. Creating the implement task and transitioning the proposal from `"ready"` to `"dispatched"` MUST be atomic; a proposal's `"dispatched"` state signals that exactly one implement task has been created for it.
    56	  - `evaluate` — `payload.trial_id` names a trial with `status == "starting"` and `commit_sha` set at creation time.
    57	- Emit the corresponding `task.created` event ( [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)) atomically with the task insert.
    58	
    59	A worker MAY rely on these preconditions ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §1.1). A task created without them is a protocol violation by the orchestrator.
    60	
    61	## 3. Claim
    62	
    63	### 3.1 Atomicity
    64	
    65	The `pending → claimed` transition MUST be atomic with respect to competing claim attempts: at most one worker MAY succeed for any given task. A conforming task store MUST provide this guarantee as part of its durability contract ([`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md)).
    66	
    67	### 3.2 Claim token
    68	
    69	A successful claim issues a `claim` object ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4) containing:
    70	
    71	- `token` — an opaque value that MUST be:
    72	  - **unique** — no two distinct claims (on the same or different tasks) share the same token within an experiment;
    73	  - **unforgeable** — a worker that did not receive the token MUST NOT be able to construct a byte-equal value with practical effort. Implementations commonly use a cryptographically random value; the protocol does not mandate the mechanism.
    74	- `worker_id` — an identifier the worker supplies at claim time. The task store MAY record it for audit but MUST NOT use it as a substitute for the token when authorizing subsequent operations.
    75	- `claimed_at` — the time the claim was issued.
    76	- `expires_at` — OPTIONAL. If present, the task store MAY reclaim the task when the current time exceeds this value (§5).
    77	
    78	### 3.3 Authorization by token
    79	
    80	Every subsequent operation on a claimed task (progress update, submit, release) MUST present the current claim token. The task store MUST reject any such operation whose token does not match the token currently recorded on the task. This is the sole mechanism the protocol relies on to guarantee single-writer access to a claimed task.
    81	
    82	### 3.4 No re-claim while claimed
    83	
    84	A conforming task store MUST reject a claim attempt against a task whose `state` is not `pending`. In particular, a claimed task cannot be "re-claimed" by a different worker; the prior claim must first be released (by submission) or invalidated (by reclamation).
    85	
    86	## 4. Submit
    87	
    88	### 4.1 Submission operation
    89	
    90	A submit operation requires:
    91	
    92	- The `task_id`.
    93	- The current claim `token`.
    94	- A result payload whose shape depends on `kind` ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4, §3.4, §4.4).
    95	
    96	A successful submit transitions `claimed → submitted` atomically with persisting the result payload. The terminal transition to `completed` or `failed` is the orchestrator's subsequent step (§4.3), even when the submission itself declared failure.
    97	
    98	### 4.2 Idempotency
    99	
   100	A resubmission is a second submit carrying the token recorded on the task's `claim` object (which is retained through `submitted` per [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4). A resubmit against a task whose claim has been cleared — because the task was reclaimed or reached a terminal state — MUST be rejected regardless of the presented token (§4.4, §5.2).
   101	
   102	When the token matches, the task store MUST handle the resubmission as follows:
   103	
   104	- If the resubmission's result payload is **content-equivalent** to the already-recorded payload, the task store MUST accept it and MUST NOT change the task's state or recorded result. "Content equivalence" means the normative fields identified per role agree:
   105	  - `plan` — the set of `proposal_ids` (compared as sets; order is not significant per [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4) and `status`.
   106	  - `implement` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `commit_sha` (when present).
   107	  - `evaluate` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `metrics` (compared as JSON values; key order does not matter).
   108	- If the resubmission's result payload is **not** content-equivalent, the task store MUST reject it. The first submission's result is the committed result.
   109	
   110	This rule exists so that a worker may safely retry a submit after a network or process failure without risk of advancing state twice or corrupting the recorded result.
   111	
   112	### 4.3 From `submitted` to terminal
   113	
   114	A submitted task is processed by the orchestrator and transitions to a terminal state. The orchestrator MUST:
   115	
   116	- Transition `submitted → completed` when the submission's `status` is `"success"` and the result satisfies the role's success contract (e.g. an implementer submission carries a reachable `commit_sha`).
   117	- Transition `submitted → failed` when either:
   118	  - The result violates the role's success contract (malformed payload, missing required field, failed post-validation).
   119	  - The worker itself declared failure: - `plan` task, worker `status == "error"` → `failed`. - `implement` task, worker `status == "error"` → `failed`. - `evaluate` task, worker `status == "error"` → `failed` AND the referenced trial's `status` MUST be updated to `"error"`. - `evaluate` task, worker `status == "eval_error"` → `failed`, but the referenced trial's `status` MUST remain `"starting"` (the trial itself was not shown to be bad; only the evaluator-side attempt failed). A fresh `evaluate` task MAY be created for the same trial; the protocol does not mandate automatic retry. If the orchestrator's policy exhausts retries for this trial (or the operator abandons it), the orchestrator MUST transition the trial's `status` from `"starting"` to `"eval_error"`, at which point the trial status is terminal.
   120	
   121	Every terminal transition produces exactly one state-change event in the log ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)) and MUST clear the task's `claim` object ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4). The orchestrator MAY implement the `claimed → submitted → completed` (or `failed`) sequence as two distinct persisted states or as a single store transaction whose event stream includes both transitions, but subscribers MUST observe both events in order.
   122	
   123	### 4.4 Post-terminal writes prohibited
   124	
   125	Once a task is `completed` or `failed`, no further writes to its task fields are permitted beyond audit-only metadata that a task store chooses to expose outside the normative schema ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.1). In particular, a resubmission against a terminal task MUST be rejected regardless of content equivalence; the claim token that produced the terminal transition is no longer valid.
   126	
   127	## 5. Reclamation
   128	
   129	### 5.1 When reclamation is permitted
   130	
   131	A conforming task store MAY reclaim a task — move it from `claimed` back to `pending` — in any of the following cases:
   132	
   133	- The `claim.expires_at` timestamp has passed.
   134	- An operator invokes an explicit reclaim action on the task.
   135	- A task-store-defined health policy determines the claiming worker is unreachable.
   136	
   137	A task in `submitted` state MAY be reclaimed **only** by an explicit operator action; automatic reclaim (expires_at, health policy) MUST NOT apply to `submitted` tasks. After submit the worker's reachability is no longer a relevant liveness signal; the orchestrator is expected to advance the task to a terminal state (§4.3).
   138	
   139	A task store MUST NOT reclaim a task in a terminal state.
   140	
   141	### 5.2 Effect of reclamation
   142	
   143	Reclamation:
   144	
   145	1. Invalidates the prior claim token. Subsequent operations presenting it MUST be rejected.
   146	2. Clears the `claim` object from the task.
   147	3. Sets the task's `state` back to `pending`.
   148	4. Is accompanied by a `task.reclaimed` event in the event log ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)), atomically with the state change.
   149	
   150	Any partial result a worker had assembled before reclamation is no longer observable through the task protocol. If the worker created store-resident objects (drafting proposals, starting trials), those objects persist per their own lifecycle and are subject to role-level cleanup rules; reclamation itself does not delete them.
   151	
   152	### 5.3 Worker detection of reclamation
   153	
   154	A worker that wishes to detect its own reclamation MAY observe its task's state or subscribe to events. If a worker discovers its token has been invalidated, it MUST discontinue work on the task and MUST NOT submit ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §1 step 5).
   155	
   156	### 5.4 Reclamation effect on role outputs
   157	
   158	When a reclaimed task had created role-owned objects during its prior execution, the orchestrator MUST reconcile those objects as follows:
   159	
   160	- **Implement reclamation.** If the prior execution created a trial with `status == "starting"`, the orchestrator MUST transition that trial's `status` to `"error"` atomically with the reclaim event. A subsequent re-claim of the implement task produces a **new** trial; starting trials are never shared across implementer attempts.
   161	- **Plan reclamation.** If the prior execution persisted proposals in `"drafting"` state, those proposals remain in `"drafting"` and are not dispatched. Implementations MAY expose them to operators for inspection or removal; the task protocol does not mandate automatic cleanup.
   162	- **Evaluate reclamation.** The trial the task referenced is unchanged by reclamation (it was produced by the implementer, not the evaluator).
   163	
   164	## 6. Ordering and concurrency
   165	
   166	### 6.1 Per-task serialization
   167	
   168	All transitions on a single task MUST be serialized: the observable history of a task is a total order. A conforming task store MUST NOT expose a state to readers that has not yet been accompanied by its event.
   169	
   170	### 6.2 Cross-task concurrency
   171	
   172	Distinct tasks MAY progress concurrently. The protocol imposes no global ordering on transitions across tasks beyond the causal order preserved by the event log ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §4.3, [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)).
   173	
   174	### 6.3 Observability
   175	
   176	A subscriber that reads the event log MUST be able to reconstruct every task's state-machine history exactly. Implementations MAY provide faster paths (direct task reads, push notifications) but MUST NOT let those paths expose states that the event log does not record.
   177	
   178	## 7. Proposal and trial lifecycle interactions
   179	
   180	The task state machine is not the only lifecycle in the system. Proposals ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5.1) and trials ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7) have their own state fields. The task protocol interacts with these lifecycles at the following points:
   181	
   182	- **Plan submit.** A successful plan-task submission MAY reference proposals that have been transitioned to `state == "ready"` by the planner ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.2). The task protocol does not itself transition proposal state; it treats the proposal as a value produced by the planner.
   183	- **Implement submit.** A successful implement-task submission requires a trial created and advanced by the implementer ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §3.2). On any terminal transition of the implement task (whether `submitted → completed` or `submitted → failed`), the orchestrator MUST transition the referenced proposal's `state` from `"dispatched"` to `"completed"`, atomically with the task's terminal event. The proposal's `"completed"` state therefore means *"the implementer's attempt on this proposal has finished"*, not *"the attempt succeeded"*; the trial's `status` field records the outcome. A proposal in `"dispatched"` always names a non-terminal implement task; no proposal remains in `"dispatched"` after its implement task has reached a terminal state.
   184	- **Evaluate submit.** A successful evaluate-task submission populates the trial's `metrics`, `completed_at`, and (per worker-declared status) the trial's `status`. The integrator's subsequent promotion of the trial into the canonical lineage is out of this chapter's scope ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)).
   185	
   186	## 8. Implementation latitude
   187	
   188	The protocol leaves to implementations:
   189	
   190	- The mechanism by which a worker discovers claimable tasks (polling, subscription, dispatch).
   191	- The representation of claim tokens, worker IDs, and task IDs.
   192	- The timeout and reclamation policy, including whether to set `expires_at` by default.
   193	- Whether retries are automatic (new task on reclamation) or operator-driven.
   194	- Whether `submitted → completed` is fully synchronous with submit or a subsequent orchestrator step, as long as the observable state machine above is preserved.
   195	
   196	What the protocol does **not** leave to implementations:
   197	
   198	- The set of states and the permitted transitions among them (§1).
   199	- The atomicity of claim (§3.1) and the token-authorization rule (§3.3).
   200	- The idempotency rule for resubmission (§4.2).
   201	- The requirement that every state change be accompanied by an atomic event (§1.3) and that the event log is the normative observability channel (§6.3).

spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:3:This chapter specifies the event log: the envelope every event MUST carry, the transactional invariant that binds event writes to the state changes they describe, the per-type payload shapes for the v0 event registry, and the delivery guarantees a conforming event log MUST offer to subscribers.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:7:## 1. Envelope
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:9:Every event appended to a conforming event log MUST be a JSON object carrying:
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:19:The envelope is closed over these fields for conforming events; a conforming log MAY persist additional audit metadata alongside each event, but MUST NOT expose it under any of the five envelope keys. Implementations MAY include additional top-level keys outside the envelope (e.g. `sequence`, `recorded_at`); subscribers relying only on the envelope MUST remain correct regardless.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:21:### 1.1 `event_id` uniqueness
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:23:`event_id` MUST be unique within the log. A subscriber MAY dedupe by `event_id` when recovering from a disconnect.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:25:### 1.2 `occurred_at` vs. log order
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:27:`occurred_at` records when the state change happened, which MAY differ from the event's position in the log — clock skew, batched writes, or retried appends can all produce non-monotonic `occurred_at` within an experiment's stream. Causal order is established by the log itself (§4.3), not by timestamps. `occurred_at` is advisory for human debugging; subscribers MUST NOT rely on it for ordering.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:29:## 2. Transactional invariant
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:33:> **Every state change that is observable via tasks, proposals, or > trials MUST be accompanied by a corresponding event, and the event > write MUST be atomic with the state change it describes.**
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:35:"Atomic" means: either both the state change and the event append are durable, or neither is. A conforming implementation MUST NOT expose a state change to readers (direct store reads, cached projections, push notifications, role dispatch) before its event is durable in the log; conversely, it MUST NOT append an event whose state change has not been durably written. If a transaction covering both is aborted, neither is observable.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:39:### 2.1 Why atomic
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:41:The event log is the normative observability channel ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §6.3). Without atomicity, subscribers reconstructing history can observe either a state without its event or an event whose state the store does not yet expose — either is a protocol violation that leaves subscribers unable to trust the log.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:43:### 2.2 Composite transitions
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:45:Several transitions span multiple entities and MUST commit together:
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:51:- **Retry-exhausted `eval_error` terminal** — the orchestrator's transition of a trial from `starting` to `eval_error` ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3). When the orchestrator persists this transition as a state change on the trial, it MUST emit `trial.eval_errored` atomically.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:52:- **Trial promotion** — the integrator's write of a `trial/*` commit and the `trial_commit_sha` field on the trial ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)). Event: `trial.integrated`.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:54:A subscriber processing any of these composite events MUST therefore either observe the full set or observe none; partial visibility is a protocol violation.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:56:### 2.3 What is *not* a state change
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:66:## 3. Event registry
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:72:### 3.1 Task events
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:93:The payload MAY include additional fields beyond those required; subscribers MUST tolerate them. A conforming orchestrator SHOULD include the submitting worker's `worker_id` on `task.submitted`, `task.completed`, and `task.failed` events when known, as an operational convenience — but this is not required because the worker-task binding is already recoverable from the preceding `task.claimed` event.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:95:### 3.2 Proposal events
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:111:### 3.3 Trial events
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:121:| `trial.integrated` | — (integrator writes `trial_commit_sha`) | `trial_id`, `trial_commit_sha` |
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:130:`trial.integrated` is not a trial-`status` transition — integration does not change the trial's `status` field, only its `trial_commit_sha`. The event marks promotion so subscribers can reconstruct the canonical lineage without reading git directly.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:132:### 3.4 Subscriber observability guarantee
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:134:Every protocol-owned state-machine transition in v0 is covered by exactly one event type in §3.1–§3.3. A subscriber that consumes every event with a registered `type`, in log order, MUST be able to reconstruct the **lifecycle history** of every task, proposal, and trial in the experiment: which entities exist, in what states, in what order. A conforming implementation MUST NOT expose a state- machine transition that is not marked by its corresponding registered event.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:136:Registered event payloads carry only the fields needed to *identify* the transitioning entity and any cross-entity references (e.g. `proposal.dispatched` carries the implement `task_id`). They do not carry full entity snapshots. A subscriber that needs the content of an entity (the proposal's `parent_commits` and `artifacts_uri`, the trial's `metrics` and `completed_at`) MUST read that entity from its store using the identifier the event carries; the read returns the entity's current state ([`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §1.7). This boundary is deliberate: events mark what happened; the entity stores hold what the entity *is*. Coupling the two into a full event-sourced projection — a subscriber reconstructing every intermediate entity value from events alone — is a deployment MAY implement on top of v0 but is not a protocol requirement, since v0 does not mandate historical reads on the entity stores.
spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:140:### 3.5 Non-registered event types
